title: Disk Saturation Guide
summary: Understand read IOPS, write IOPS, average I/O size, throughput capacity, and target disk utilization.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates storage saturation risk by combining IOPS demand with average I/O size and available throughput capacity.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch performance pressure before it becomes production trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when storage latency, database responsiveness, VM performance, logging, or application throughput may be limited by disk activity.
bullets: Early performance planning.
Expansion or load-growth review.
Existing-system troubleshooting or validation.
Explaining performance tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible inputs on this tool and explain what each value represents in practical performance planning terms.

items.label: Read IOPS
items.description: The expected number of read operations per second.
items.whyItMatters: Read IOPS drives random-read pressure and can consume queue depth even when throughput looks acceptable.
items.commonMistake: Assuming cache will eliminate read pressure under every workload.

links.label: Compare disk pressure to bottleneck analysis
links.note: Use this when disk pressure competes with CPU or network limits.

items.label: Write IOPS
items.description: The expected number of write operations per second.
items.whyItMatters: Writes often create more sustained pressure than reads because they may involve journaling, parity, replication, or flush behavior.
items.commonMistake: Underestimating writes because each operation is small in capacity terms.

items.label: Avg IO Size (KB)
items.description: The average size of each storage operation.
items.whyItMatters: I/O size converts IOPS into MB/s demand. Larger operations can saturate throughput even at lower IOPS.
items.commonMistake: Comparing IOPS ratings without matching the block size.

items.label: Disk Throughput Capacity (MB/s)
items.description: The sustained usable throughput capacity of the disk path.
items.whyItMatters: This is the practical ceiling the combined read/write workload is compared against.
items.commonMistake: Using manufacturer peak numbers instead of sustained workload throughput.

links.label: Translate capacity into margin
links.note: Capacity needs a utilization target before it becomes a safe limit.

items.label: Target Utilization (%)
items.description: The maximum disk utilization you want to allow during normal operation.
items.whyItMatters: A target utilization leaves room for bursts, rebuilds, snapshots, and background maintenance.
items.commonMistake: Running storage near full utilization and expecting latency to stay predictable.

links.label: Understand utilization margin
links.note: Disk utilization targets are a headroom decision.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means the disk path has usable performance margin. Watch means I/O size, write pressure, or capacity assumptions deserve review. Risk means storage may become a bottleneck and raise latency across the system.
bullets: Healthy: the entered assumptions leave practical operating margin.
Watch: the design may still work, but the assumption deserves review before relying on it.
Risk: reduce demand, increase capacity, change the architecture, or validate the design more carefully before implementation.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most performance planning mistakes come from looking at one symptom in isolation instead of tracing how latency, throughput, queues, concurrency, and resource pressure interact.
bullets: Sizing for average behavior while ignoring peak periods, bursts, retries, and background work.
Tuning one layer without checking whether the bottleneck simply moves to another resource.
Treating theoretical maximums as safe sustained production capacity.
Failing to document the assumptions behind latency targets, utilization limits, and growth margin.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace application testing, manufacturer documentation, platform-specific limits, monitoring data, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Measurements from the real platform are better than guesses whenever they are available.
Application behavior, storage design, network path, cache behavior, and workload mix can change the result.
Healthy results still need monitoring and validation after deployment.
A design with documented headroom is usually easier to support, explain, and grow.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, production testing, vendor validation, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual hardware, software, platform, and network path being deployed.
Confirm stakeholder expectations, operating requirements, and SLA language.
Document assumptions before implementation and revisit them as traffic or workload behavior changes.
